section.title: Those Who Trust in the Lord
section.sectionRationale: A Song of Ascents, sixth in the pilgrim collection Psalms 120 to 134, placed in Book V of the Psalter, Psalms 107 to 150. Just as Mount Zion's surrounding hills form a natural wall around Jerusalem, this psalm pictures the LORD Himself surrounding His trusting people with unshakeable protection.
section.thesis: Those who trust in the LORD are as immovable as Mount Zion, kept secure by His surrounding presence and His just limits on the reign of the wicked, a security and righteousness fulfilled in Christ who holds His people fast and will never let them be snatched away.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the immovable security of those who trust the LORD, verses 1 to 2, the limit set on the scepter of the wicked, verse 3, and the closing prayer for the righteous and a blessing of peace, verses 4 to 5.

verses.text: Those who {{batach|trust}} in the LORD are like Mount Zion. It cannot be moved; it abides forever.
As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds His people, both now and forevermore.
For the scepter of the wicked will not rest upon the land allotted to the righteous, so that the righteous will not put forth their hands to injustice.
Do good, O LORD, to those who are good, and to the upright in heart.
But those who turn to crooked ways the LORD will banish with the evildoers. Peace be upon Israel.

batach.gloss: To trust, feel secure, rely confidently on someone; here the ground of an unshakeable stability, like Mount Zion itself, given to those who trust the LORD.

basic.context: This Song of Ascents pictures Jerusalem's surrounding hills as a portrait of the LORD's protection of His trusting people, and prays that His just rule, not the scepter of the wicked, would govern the land allotted to the righteous.
basic.hermeneutics: It describes Israel's confidence in the LORD's protection and just governance over her land, and it prescribes as an abiding pattern trusting the LORD as an unshakeable foundation and praying for His people's uprightness and peace.
basic.originalLanguages: The psalm opens with batach, trust, the confident reliance that grounds the whole picture of Mount Zion's immovable stability, and closes with shalom, peace, upon Israel, echoing Psalm 122's prayer.
basic.typology: Mount Zion's immovable stability anticipates the security believers have in Christ, who holds His own so that none can snatch them from His hand, and the limited scepter of the wicked anticipates the final judgment when every unrighteous rule gives way to Christ's eternal reign.
basic.christ: Jesus promises that no one will snatch His sheep out of His hand, John 10:28-29, the fulfillment of the security this psalm pictures in Mount Zion, and He is the one whose righteous scepter will finally and fully displace every wicked reign, Hebrews 1:8.
basic.god: The LORD surrounds His trusting people the way mountains ring Jerusalem, guarding them now and forever, and He sets a just limit on the rule of the wicked so that His righteous ones are not driven to injustice by prolonged oppression.
basic.crossReferences: John 10:28-29 shows Christ holding His people fast so none can snatch them away. Matthew 7:24-25 pictures the house built on the rock, unmoved by storm, as this psalm pictures Zion. Hebrews 1:8 sets Christ's righteous scepter over every fading reign of wickedness.
basic.oneStory: This psalm's picture of unshakeable trust and a limited scepter of wickedness is fulfilled in Christ, who holds His people utterly secure and whose righteous, eternal reign will finally displace every unjust power.

context.literary: A Song of Ascents built on a single extended image, Jerusalem's surrounding mountains, moving from the security of the trusting to a prayer that the LORD's own justice, not the rule of the wicked, would govern His people's land.
context.historical: Likely composed in a season when foreign or oppressive rule pressed upon Israel's land, prompting the prayer that such a 'scepter of the wicked' not become a permanent fixture provoking the righteous to compromise.
context.canonical: Follows Psalm 124's memory of a past deliverance with a present confidence in ongoing protection, and anticipates the New Testament's picture of believers held secure in Christ against every hostile power.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To assure God's people that trusting the LORD makes them as stable as Mount Zion, and to pray that His just rule, not prolonged wicked power, would govern the land, keeping the righteous from being provoked into wrongdoing.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The psalm describes Israel's historic confidence in the LORD's protection over Jerusalem and her land. It prescribes as an abiding pattern trusting the LORD as an unshakeable foundation and praying that oppression not tempt God's people into compromise.

debates.question: Does 'the scepter of the wicked' in verse 3 refer to a specific foreign occupier or to wicked rule in general?

views.label: A specific historical oppressor
views.case: The concrete language of a scepter resting on allotted land fits a particular season of foreign or unjust domination over Israel's territory.

views.label: A general principle about wicked rule
views.case: The psalm gives no names or dates, suggesting a broader principle: God limits how long unjust power may weigh on His people, whoever the oppressor.

debates.bearing: Either reading affirms the same pastoral comfort, that God sets real limits on injustice and will not let it permanently corrupt His righteous people.

study.originalLanguages: Trust, batach, opens the psalm and grounds its central image, that those who rely on the LORD are as immovable as Mount Zion. The psalm closes with shalom, peace, over Israel, the same wholeness and security prayed for in Psalm 122.

typology.type: Mount Zion, immovable and abiding forever, verse 1
typology.fulfillment: Believers held secure in Christ, from whose hand no one can snatch them, John 10:28-29.

typology.type: The limited scepter of the wicked, verse 3
typology.fulfillment: Every wicked reign gives way at last to Christ's righteous and eternal scepter, Hebrews 1:8.

study.christ: Jesus declares that no one will snatch His sheep out of His hand, nor out of the Father's hand, John 10:28-29, the deeper fulfillment of the immovable security this psalm pictures in Mount Zion. He is also the King whose righteous scepter, unlike every fading wicked reign, will stand forever, Hebrews 1:8, the final answer to this psalm's prayer that injustice not have the last word.
study.god: The LORD surrounds His trusting people the way the mountains ring Jerusalem, guarding them now and forevermore, and He limits how long the scepter of the wicked may rest upon His people's land, so that the righteous are not driven by prolonged oppression into wrongdoing.

crossReferences.note: Christ holds His people fast so that none can snatch them from His hand, fulfilling this psalm's picture of immovable security.
The house built on the rock stands unmoved by storm, echoing this psalm's image of unshakeable trust.
Christ's righteous scepter stands forever, the final answer to every limited and fading wicked reign.

study.oneStory: This psalm's picture of unshakeable trust in the LORD and a limited scepter of wickedness is fulfilled in Christ, who holds His people utterly secure and whose righteous, eternal reign will finally displace every unjust power over the earth.

sermon.title: Three Songs of Faith
sermon.why: Begg treats Psalms 123 to 125 together, showing how this psalm's picture of Zion's immovable stability grounds the pilgrim's confidence in the LORD's surrounding protection.

quiz.q: What are those who trust in the LORD compared to in verse 1?

options.text: Mount Zion, which cannot be moved and abides forever.
options.note: This is the psalm's central image of unshakeable stability.

options.text: A reed shaken by the wind.
options.note: This is the opposite image; the psalm stresses immovability, not fragility.

options.text: A ship tossed on stormy seas.
options.note: The psalm's picture is stability, not being tossed about.

options.text: A tent that can be quickly relocated.
options.note: Close to the theme of dwelling, but the specific image given is a fixed, immovable mountain.

quiz.q: How does verse 2 describe the LORD's protection of His people?

options.text: As the mountains surround Jerusalem, so the LORD surrounds His people.
options.note: This is the exact simile the psalm uses for the LORD's encompassing protection.

options.text: As a distant observer who rarely intervenes.
options.note: The psalm pictures constant, encompassing nearness, not distance.

options.text: As a single wall built on one side of the city.
options.note: The image is of surrounding mountains on every side, not one wall.

options.text: As a river that occasionally floods the land.
options.note: Rivers and floods appear in Psalm 124, not this psalm's protective imagery.

quiz.q: What does verse 3 say about the scepter of the wicked?

options.text: It will not rest permanently on the land allotted to the righteous.
options.note: This is the psalm's assurance that unjust rule over God's people has a limit.

options.text: It will rule forever without end.
options.note: The psalm promises the opposite, a limit on wicked rule.

options.text: It will be handed over willingly by the righteous.
options.note: The text speaks of God limiting the wicked's rule, not the righteous surrendering power.

options.text: It will only affect foreign nations, never Israel.
options.note: The verse specifically concerns the land allotted to the righteous, Israel's own territory.

quiz.q: What concern does verse 3 raise about prolonged wicked rule?

options.text: That the righteous might be provoked to stretch out their hands to do wrong.
options.note: This is the specific danger the psalm names, injustice tempting even the righteous to compromise.

options.text: That the righteous would become wealthy.
options.note: Wealth is not the concern raised; moral compromise under oppression is.

options.text: That the land would become physically barren.
options.note: The concern is moral and spiritual, not agricultural.

options.text: That foreign trade would decline.
options.note: Trade is not mentioned; the concern is the righteous being tempted toward wrongdoing.

quiz.q: How does the New Testament pick up this psalm's picture of unshakeable security?

options.text: Jesus promises that no one can snatch His sheep out of His hand.
options.note: John 10:28-29 gives the deeper fulfillment of the immovable security pictured in Mount Zion.

options.text: It teaches that believers can never feel secure in this life.
options.note: The New Testament offers strong assurance of security in Christ, the opposite of this claim.

options.text: It limits this security to angels alone.
options.note: John 10 explicitly applies this security to Christ's sheep, His people, not angels.

options.text: It replaces security with a call to relentless anxiety.
options.note: This misrepresents the New Testament's consistent call to confident trust in Christ's keeping.

questions: The psalm compares those who trust the LORD to an immovable mountain. Where in your life do you feel unstable, and how might deeper trust in the LORD steady you there?
The LORD surrounds His people the way mountains ring Jerusalem, on every side. Do you experience God's protection as all-encompassing, or only in certain areas of your life?
The psalm worries that prolonged injustice might provoke the righteous to compromise. Where are you tempted to do wrong because oppression or unfairness has gone on too long?
The psalm prays for good to come to the upright in heart. What would it look like for you to actively pray this blessing over fellow believers this week?
Jesus promises that no one can snatch His sheep from His hand. How does that promise change the way you face threats to your faith or your security?

translationNotes.note: Trust, batach, conveys confident reliance, not mere hope; it is the ground for the psalm's picture of Zion's immovable stability.
Scepter can also be rendered rod or staff, an image of ruling authority; its 'resting' pictures prolonged, settled occupation rather than a brief raid.
Peace be upon Israel closes the psalm with shalom, the same wholeness and security prayed for Jerusalem in Psalm 122:6-9.

meta.notes: BSB text taken verbatim from biblehub.com's BSB page for Psalm 125 (the local makor-scripture toolkit returned a disk I/O error, so the web fallback was used for all of Psalms 124-132). Verse count (5) and wording cross-checked against the section map bounds Psalms 125:1-5. No em or en dashes present in the source text. Sermon link verified this session via WebSearch and web_fetch confirming Alistair Begg's sermon 'Three Songs of Faith' (covering Psalms 123-125) at truthforlife.org.
